_metadata.description: Cognitive Load Theory — memory model, three load types (intrinsic/extraneous/germane), dual-channel theory, problem space, Canvas audit indicators for load-type tagging.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~7,058 chars / 4 ≈ 1,765 tokens — within the 'embed' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Override to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 knowledge files per audit task from a pool of 12, not all of them as a stable prefix. Selective-load consumers don't benefit from prompt caching of an embedded prefix because the prefix changes per call.

scope: Cognitive Load Theory applied to Canvas course design audits. Supplies the working-memory mechanics that determine whether any Hattie phase can succeed. Boundaries: does not cover sequencing across phases (see hattie_3phase_knowledge) or domain coverage (see three_domains_knowledge).

facts.definition: Incoming information flows through sensory memory → working memory → long-term memory. Working memory is the bottleneck — it processes 5–9 chunks at a time. Long-term memory stores information in schemas that compress prior knowledge into a single retrievable chunk.

Organized long-term-memory structures that compress prior knowledge into a single retrievable chunk. A well-developed schema, no matter how complex, counts as one chunk in working memory — which is why experts can hold and manipulate ideas a novice cannot.
The inherent difficulty of the material; sourced from content complexity. Cannot be reduced without changing the outcome — but can be sequenced (simpler → harder).
citations: Sweller (1988); MCW Office of Educational Improvement (May 2022)

facts.definition: Effort spent fighting the presentation rather than the content; sourced from poor design. Eliminate first — this is the load designers have direct control over.
citations: Sweller (1988); MCW Office of Educational Improvement (May 2022)

facts.definition: Productive cognitive work that builds schemas; sourced from learning activity (discussion, comparison, application). Encourage — this is the work of understanding.
citations: Sweller (1988); MCW Office of Educational Improvement (May 2022)

facts.definition: Intrinsic + extraneous + germane > working memory capacity. Even capable learners fail when the total exceeds the limit. Priority for designers: reduce extraneous first.

Working memory has two separate processing channels (auditory and visual) that do not compete. Pairing a visual diagram with spoken narration expands total capacity; pairing a diagram with on-screen text duplicates the visual channel and reduces capacity (split-attention effect).
citations: Mayer multimedia learning principles; MCW 2022 guide

facts.definition: The gap between a learner's current state and the desired goal. Too small → boring, no learning. Too large → overload, no learning. Right size → progress, with effort. Effective scaffolding shrinks the problem space by chunking complex tasks into ordered sub-tasks that each fit in working memory.

Eliminate these: >7 items in a module; inconsistent module/item naming; duplicate or contradictory instructions; labels separated from diagrams; unpublished items in a published module; no time estimate; required external tool with no direct link; module starts mid-task with no overview.
citations: Compiled in this knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators section

facts.definition: Sequence, don't reduce: hard concept introduced before prerequisites; multiple new concepts in one item; assessment requires combining skills not yet practiced individually.
Protect and encourage: module has only readings + a quiz; discussions with no synthesis prompt; all assessments are recall-only; no reflection or self-assessment.

Every audit issue carries a cognitive_load_type field with value: extraneous (highest priority — fix first), intrinsic (medium), or germane (medium-high). Pair with the hattie_phase tag for a full diagnosis: what kind of load is blocking which phase of learning.

sources: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning. Cognitive Science, 12(2), 257–285.
Atkinson, R.C. & Shiffrin, R.M. (1968) memory model.
Cognitive Load Theory: A Guide to Applying CLT to Your Teaching. Medical College of Wisconsin, Office of Educational Improvement, May 2022.

pre_run_checklist: Each entry in consumed_by[] resolves to an existing agent spec OR is being generated in the same session
post_run_checklist: knowledge/cognitive_load_theory_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and facts[] present

changes: Retrofit: JSON companion authored for pre-existing canvas-toolbox knowledge MD.
MD body and canvas-toolbox header convention (Source:/Used by:/Companion:) preserved per make_agent_knowledge.md Retrofit Mode step 3 and P-007 (Pull Don't Push).
Runtime strategy set to read_at_runtime via named_override_patterns.selective_load (canvas_course_expert audit loads 2-3 of 12 knowledge files per task).
